name: Electromagnetic Waves
subject: Physics

sections.heading: 1. Displacement Current
sections.body:
Maxwell noticed that Ampere's law $\oint\vec{B}\cdot d\vec{l} = \mu_0 I$ fails in cases like the gap of a charging capacitor — current flows in the wires but not in the gap, yet a magnetic field exists there.

Maxwell introduced the displacement current: $$I_d = \varepsilon_0\dfrac{d\Phi_E}{dt}$$ where $\Phi_E$ is the electric flux. The modified Ampere-Maxwell law: $$\oint\vec{B}\cdot d\vec{l} = \mu_0(I_c + I_d) = \mu_0 I_c + \mu_0\varepsilon_0\dfrac{d\Phi_E}{dt}$$ where $I_c$ is conduction current. In the capacitor gap, $I_d = I_c$, ensuring continuity.

This was the missing piece that completed Maxwell's equations and predicted EM waves.

sections.heading: 2. Maxwell's Equations and EM Waves
sections.body:
The four Maxwell's equations in free space:

  1. $\oint\vec{E}\cdot d\vec{A} = q/\varepsilon_0$ (Gauss for electricity)
  2. $\oint\vec{B}\cdot d\vec{A} = 0$ (Gauss for magnetism — no monopoles)
  3. $\oint\vec{E}\cdot d\vec{l} = -d\Phi_B/dt$ (Faraday)
  4. $\oint\vec{B}\cdot d\vec{l} = \mu_0\varepsilon_0\,d\Phi_E/dt$ (Ampere-Maxwell, with $I_c = 0$)

These predict the existence of self-sustaining electromagnetic waves: a changing $\vec{E}$ creates $\vec{B}$, which in turn creates $\vec{E}$, and the wave propagates through vacuum at speed $$c = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_0\varepsilon_0}} \approx 3 \times 10^8\,\text{m/s}$$ Match with measured speed of light → light is an EM wave (Hertz, 1888 confirmed experimentally).

sections.heading: 3. Nature and Properties of EM Waves
sections.body:
Properties of electromagnetic waves:

  • Transverse: $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{B}$ are mutually perpendicular and both perpendicular to direction of propagation $\hat{k}$. Order: $\vec{E}\times\vec{B}$ points along $\hat{k}$.
  • Travel through vacuum (no medium needed).
  • Speed in vacuum: $c$. In medium: $v = 1/\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon} = c/n$, where $n$ is refractive index.
  • Sinusoidal plane wave: $E_y = E_0\sin(kx - \omega t)$, $B_z = B_0\sin(kx - \omega t)$. At every point and instant: $E_0/B_0 = c$.
  • Carry energy and momentum. Average energy density: $u = \tfrac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 E_0^2 = B_0^2/(2\mu_0)$ — equally split between $E$ and $B$.
  • Intensity: $I = \tfrac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 c E_0^2$.
  • Exert radiation pressure: $p = U/c$ (absorbed) or $2U/c$ (reflected), where $U$ is energy delivered.

sections.heading: 4. Electromagnetic Spectrum
sections.body:
EM waves span an enormous range of frequencies (and wavelengths $\lambda = c/f$). All travel at $c$ in vacuum but interact with matter very differently.

From low to high frequency:

  • Radio waves (>0.1 m): radio/TV, broadcast. Generated by oscillating LC circuits, antennas. Detected by tuned LC.
  • Microwaves (~1 mm – 0.1 m): radar, communication, microwave ovens (water rotational excitation). Generated by klystrons, magnetrons.
  • Infrared (~700 nm – 1 mm): heat radiation, remote controls, IR photography, greenhouse effect. Produced by hot bodies, molecular vibrations.
  • Visible light (~400–700 nm): VIBGYOR. Detected by eye. Source of vision.
  • Ultraviolet (~10 – 400 nm): from sun, sterilisation, fluorescence; absorbed by ozone — protects life.
  • X-rays (~0.01 – 10 nm): medical imaging, crystallography. Generated by deceleration of high-energy electrons (Bremsstrahlung).
  • Gamma rays (<0.01 nm): from nuclear transitions and astrophysical sources; cancer therapy. Highest energy and most penetrating.

summary: Maxwell's displacement current $I_d = \varepsilon_0 d\Phi_E/dt$ completed Ampere's law and predicted EM waves traveling at $c = 1/\sqrt{\mu_0\varepsilon_0}$. EM waves are transverse, with $\vec{E}\perp\vec{B}\perp\hat{k}$, $E_0/B_0 = c$. Energy is split equally between $E$ and $B$ fields. Spectrum spans radio → microwave → IR → visible → UV → X-ray → gamma rays, all governed by $\lambda f = c$.
pitfalls: Displacement current does not involve actual charge motion — it's the rate of change of electric flux.
EM waves are transverse, not longitudinal (sound is longitudinal — confused).
E and B are in phase (peak together), not 90° out — a common error.
Energy density is split equally between E and B fields, not all in E.
All EM waves travel at SAME speed c in vacuum, regardless of frequency.
Radiation pressure formula differs by factor 2 between absorption and reflection.
